issues.body: `os.open(path, O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, 0600)` only applies `0600` when creating a new file. If `path` already names a mode-0644 file, `json.dump` writes the access token while that inode remains readable, and only afterward does `os.chmod` narrow its mode. The open also follows symlinks, allowing a stale or manipulated output path to redirect truncation and secret output to another file. Thus the implementation does not actually guarantee that the capability goes only to a private file. The self-test covers only a newly created destination, so it cannot catch either failure mode.
issues.fix_hint: Create the output fail-closed without following symlinks (for example, use an atomic private temporary file in the destination directory and replace after fsync, with appropriate destination validation, or use `O_EXCL|O_NOFOLLOW` and reject an existing path). Add non-vacuous tests for an existing permissive destination and a symlink destination, asserting that no capability bytes become readable through or written to the unsafe target.
issues.title: Capability is written before an existing destination is made private

verdict.summary: The live capability is no longer printed, and the new tests exercise the ordinary file-output and missing-destination paths. However, the file writer does not preserve the claimed private-file invariant when the caller-supplied destination already exists: it writes through the existing inode (and follows symlinks) before applying chmod. That can expose or overwrite the token-bearing capability through a permissive or redirected destination, so this trust-sensitive change is not yet fail-closed.
